g.data-narration: Welcome! Today we explore how multidimensional arrays are stored in computer memory - a crucial concept for programming and optimization.
Computer memory is a linear sequence of bytes. But arrays like matrices are multidimensional. We need to map 2D structure onto 1D memory.
g.text: Computer memory is fundamentally ONE-DIMENSIONAL - a linear sequence of bytes.
But we need 2D, 3D... nD arrays. How do we map them?

g.data-narration: A 2D array has a logical matrix view with rows and columns. In physical memory, all 12 elements must be placed sequentially.
Row-major order stores elements row by row. First entire row 0, then row 1, then row 2. C, C++, and Java use this method.
g.text: Elements stored row by row: entire first row, then second row, then third.
Used by: C, C++, Java, Pascal

g.data-narration: Column-major order stores elements column by column. First entire column 0, then column 1. FORTRAN and MATLAB use this approach.
g.text: Elements stored column by column: entire first column, then second column.

g.data-narration: The row-major address formula is: Address = B + (i × N + j) × w. Each term has specific meaning for calculating element locations.
g.text: i = row index, j = col index
N = columns, B = base, w = element size

g.data-narration: Column-major formula: Address = B + (j × M + i) × w. Notice we multiply column index by rows, then add row index.
g.text: j = column index, i = row index
M = rows, B = base, w = element size

g.data-narration: Let's calculate: A[3][4] at B=1000, int=4 bytes. Find A[2][1]. Using formula: 1000 + (2×4+1)×4 = 1036.
Same array, same element A[2][1] in column-major: 1000 + (1×3+2)×4 = 1020. Notice different address than row-major!
g.text: Key insight: Same element (A[2][1]) at 1036 (row-major) vs 1020 (column-major)!

g.data-narration: For 1-based indexing (common in exams), subtract 1 from each index. Row-major: B + ((i-1)×N + (j-1))×w.
For 3D arrays A[X][Y][Z], the row-major formula extends to three dimensions: Address = B + (i×Y×Z + j×Z + k)×w.
g.text: Row-major: B + (i × Y×Z + j × Z + k) × w

g.data-narration: Cache performance matters! Row-major traversal in C accesses memory sequentially (fast). Column-major traversal causes cache misses (slow).
g.text: Sequential access = cache hits | Strided access = cache misses

g.data-narration: Compare row-major vs column-major: storage order, languages, formulas, and which traversal is cache-friendly in each language.
Exam tips: memorize all four formulas. Identify 0-based vs 1-based indexing. Remember C always uses row-major order.
g.text: 4. Make rightmost index vary fastest for cache efficiency

g.data-narration: Key takeaways: memory is 1D but arrays are multidimensional. Two mapping strategies exist. Choose traversal order based on storage order.
g.text: Key Takeaway: Memory is 1D - map nD arrays using Row-Major or Column-Major order
